options.text: They both generate new images at the same time
The generator creates new images, and the discriminator tries to detect if they are fake
The discriminator generates images, and the generator rates them

exercises.prompt: In a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN), how do the generator and discriminator work together?
exercises.explain: A generator creates new content, while a discriminator acts as a critic to evaluate if it is real or fake.

cards.title: Who Really Made This?
cards.body: Imagine posting an amazing AI-generated artwork online, only for someone else to claim they painted it by hand. How can we prove a machine made it without ruining the beautiful image with a giant, ugly stamp?

AI watermarking is the practice of embedding hidden, mathematical signatures directly into the pixels of generated media. It is invisible to the human eye but easily read by detection software, allowing platforms to attribute the image to the AI model that created it.
cards.title: Invisible Digital Signatures

cards.body: Think of it like lemon juice writing. To anyone looking at the paper, it seems completely blank. But if you hold it over a light bulb or apply a special chemical, the hidden writing appears. An AI watermark is like that secret ink, hidden inside the colors of the image.
cards.title: The Lemon Juice Secret
Tagging the Cityscape
cards.body: An AI platform generates a photo of a futuristic city. Before showing it to the user, the AI subtly tweaks a few pixel values in a repeating pattern. When uploaded to a social network, the site's scanner spots the pattern instantly and adds a label: 'Generated with AI'.

cards.title: Can You Just Crop It Out?
cards.body: Many believe that taking a screenshot or cropping an AI image will destroy the watermark. In reality, modern AI watermarks are designed to survive resizing, cropping, compression, and even screenshotting, because the signature is woven deep into the texture of the image.
See how a watermark looks to humans versus how a detection computer scans it.

right.text: A bright, highlighted grid pattern of hidden data.

cards.title: Hidden in Plain Sight

options.text: It embeds invisible signatures in the pixels to trace the image's source
It puts a bright red logo in the corner of every generated picture
It deletes the image if someone tries to copy it without permission

exercises.prompt: What is the primary purpose of embedding a watermark in a generated image?
exercises.explain: Watermarking hides a digital signature inside the pixel values, allowing computers to identify it as AI-generated without changing how it looks to us.
AI watermarks are designed to be completely invisible to the human eye, preserving the beauty of the artwork.
exercises.prompt: True or False: AI watermarks must be large and visible to prevent people from copying images.

exercises.sentence: AI watermarks hide information by making tiny, invisible changes directly to the image's [blank] .
exercises.prompt: AI watermarks hide information by making tiny, invisible changes directly to the image's _____ .
exercises.explain: Watermarking algorithms subtly adjust the values of individual pixels to encode identification data without ruining the image.
The second scenario features a hidden pixel pattern specifically designed to be read by detection algorithms, which is how AI watermarks function.

options.text: An artist paints their initials in the bottom right corner of a digital canvas
An AI generator modifies light patterns in a photo so a detector can identify its origin

exercises.prompt: Which of these shows an example of AI watermarking?

exercises.explain: Watermarks allow platforms to automatically detect and flag synthetic media, promoting transparency and trust across the web. Next up, we will look at how GANs are evaluated and tested!

options.text: By training a new GAN to paint over the watermark
By scanning the media for the hidden signature to label it as AI-generated
By deleting any image that does not have a visible copyright symbol

exercises.prompt: How do social media platforms use invisible watermarks to stop the spread of unlabeled fake images?

title: Watermarking and Attribution
concept: Intentionally embedding invisible digital watermarks in generated images lets platforms identify synthetic media and track its source.
